The difference in Josiah's early succession compared to Manasseh’s must be attributed to God’s special grace; yet it is likely that those who raised him were instruments in producing this difference. His character was excellent. If the people had joined in the reformation as wholeheartedly as he persevered in it, positive results would have followed. But they were wicked and had become foolish through idolatry. We do not fully understand the state of Judah from historical records unless we refer to the writings of the prophets who lived at that time. While repairing the temple, the book of the law was found and brought to the king. It seems this book of the law was lost and missing; carelessly misplaced and neglected, as some put their Bibles in corners or maliciously concealed by idolaters. God’s care for the Bible clearly shows his interest in it. Whether this was the only copy available or not, the things contained within were new to both the king and the high priest. No summaries, extracts, or collections from the Bible can convey and preserve knowledge of God and his will like the Bible itself. It is no surprise that the people were so corrupt when the book of the law was scarce; those who corrupted them likely used methods to keep it out of their hands. The abundance of Bibles we possess increases our national sins; for what greater contempt of God can we show than to refuse to read his word when given to us, or, reading it, not to believe and obey? By the holy law is knowledge of sin, and by the blessed gospel is knowledge of salvation. When the former is understood in its strictness and excellence, the sinner begins to ask, What must I do to be saved? And ministers of the gospel point out Jesus Christ as the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes.

The book of the law is read before the king. Those who best honor their Bibles study them; they daily feed on that bread and walk by that light. Convictions of sin and wrath should lead us to ask, What shall we do to be saved? Also, what we may expect and must prepare for. Those who are truly aware of the weight of God’s wrath cannot help but be very anxious about how they may be saved. Huldah told Josiah what judgments God had in store for Judah and Jerusalem. Most of the people were hardened and their hearts unhumbled, but Josiah's heart was tender. This is tenderness of heart, and in this way he humbled himself before the Lord. Those who most fear God’s wrath are least likely to experience it. Though Josiah was mortally wounded in battle, he died in peace with God and went to glory. Whatever such people suffer or witness, they are gathered to their graves in peace and will enter into the rest that remains for the people of God.
